_meta.purpose: Config-for-one content data contract: the Jeff-specific, stable rendered-site content extracted from compare-purple-gold.html. Single place to review/edit site content. The canonical HTML is still authoritative for RENDER (no build step yet); tests/test_content_contract.py guards this contract against drift from the HTML.
_meta.excludes:
Cron-managed field-of-interest spans (wire_letter_writers.py owns docs/letter_writers.json -> the .field-of-interest

in peer/collaborator/mentor cards) and per-person shared-publication lists. Those are volatile and Producer-Owned; this contract captures only the stable identity/content fields so the drift guard stays green across cron runs.
_meta.next_increment_jeff_gated: Flip the canonical page to RENDER from this contract (generator/build step). That crosses the documented no-build-system constraint, so it is a direction call for Jeff, not autonomous.

meta.title: Roizen Lab | Vitamin D Research | hypothesisdriven.org
meta.description: The Roizen Lab at UPenn and CHOP studies the non-calcium benefits of vitamin D — immune modulation, metabolic regulation, and new therapies for common diseases like sarcopenia and PCOS.
meta.og_description: Rethinking vitamin D. The Roizen Lab explores non-calcium effects of vitamin D and translates them into new therapies.
meta.twitter_description: The Roizen Lab at UPenn and CHOP studies the non-calcium benefits of vitamin D and translates them into new therapies.

hero.heading: Redefining Vitamin D
hero.subtitle: Vitamin D is one of the most recommended therapies in medicine — and one of the least understood. We are changing that.
hero.affiliations: University of Pennsylvania · Children's Hospital of Philadelphia

big_questions.section_intro: Each project in our lab is driven by a fundamental unanswered question about vitamin D with clear clinical relevance.

questions.title: Low Vitamin D: Cause or Effect?
questions.body: Low vitamin D is associated with many diseases. Medicine assumed low vitamin D was causative. We used disease models and observational genetic studies to ask whether the opposite was true: does disease cause low vitamin D? In asthma, obesity, and aging, the answer was yes. Disease lowers vitamin D — not the other way around.

questions.title: How Does Disease Lower Vitamin D?
questions.body: Sick patients almost always have low vitamin D. Why? We measured hepatic 25-hydroxylase activity across disease states. Obesity and aging directly suppress the liver enzyme that activates vitamin D. If biology varies by disease, does it also vary by person?

questions.title: Same Dose, Different Results
questions.body: Two patients take the same dose. One responds. One doesn't. Why? We studied coding SNPs in CYP2R1 — the gene for vitamin D's activating enzyme. Genetic variation shapes who benefits from supplementation and who doesn't. The person matters as much as the pill.

questions.title: Is the Dose the Drug?
questions.body: Most trials give pharmacologic doses to people who aren't deficient. That's not the same biology as correcting a deficiency. High-dose vitamin D activates pathways that replacement doses don't touch. The dose isn't just a number — it changes what the molecule does.

questions.title: How Does High-Dose D Prevent T2 Diabetes?
questions.body: Large trials show high-dose vitamin D reduces diabetes risk in prediabetic patients. The effect is real. The mechanism is unknown. We're working to identify which metabolic pathways mediate the protective effect — and whether vitamin D redirects energy metabolism more broadly.

questions.title: What Determines Calorie Allocation?
questions.body: Your body decides whether surplus calories become fat, muscle, or growth. What controls that decision? We built CYP2R1 knockout mice — animals that can't activate vitamin D. Without it, calories go to fat. With it, they go to muscle and linear growth. Vitamin D is a metabolic traffic cop.

questions.title: The Missing Biomarker
questions.body: PTH tells us when vitamin D is adequate for bones. Nothing tells us when it's adequate for metabolism. Every clinical trial in the field is limited by this gap. We're searching for a blood marker that tracks vitamin D's non-calcium effects. Finding one would change how we dose, monitor, and study vitamin D.

join_card.heading: Join Our Team
join_card.body: We currently do not have hard funding for additional personnel, but are always looking to identify curious, enthusiastic, hard-working people who want to do careful, ambitious science — the kind that moves the boundaries of knowledge, even if only by a few millimeters.

publications.tagline: Selected highlights, organized by research arc. The complete, auto-updated list appears below.

items.title: New preprint: High-dose vitamin D reallocates surplus calories from fat to muscle
items.body: Our latest work shows that dietary vitamin D modulates myostatin and leptin signaling to redirect caloric surplus toward lean mass and linear growth.

items.title: New preprint: Vitamin D modulates tumor engraftment through GC protein
items.body: We show that dietary vitamin D regulates GC protein (vitamin D binding protein) abundance, with implications for tumor biology.

items.title: Alex Casella defends PhD and matches into Neurology at Johns Hopkins
items.body: Congratulations to Alex on completing her MD/PhD and beginning residency training at Hopkins.

items.title: "Vitamin D Therapy and the Era of Precision Medicine" published in JCEM
items.body: Our perspective on how genetic variation in vitamin D metabolism should reshape clinical practice.

donate.body_primary: Your donation buys reagents, mice, and people-hours. That's it.
donate.body_secondary: All donations are tax-deductible and processed through the Children's Hospital of Philadelphia Foundation.
